text: Hello, my name is Alin Burlaku and I am a young thoracic surgeon at Institute of Oncology Bucharest, Romania. In the next few minutes, I will present you a case of uniportal video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery left lower lobe radical lobectomy.

Our patient is a forty-four-year-old woman with breast cancer multimodal treated. Patient's surgical history is represented by modern mastectomy on the left side and a left lower lobe wedge resection through mini-thoracotomy.

pathological report lung adenocarcinoma during oncological surveillance a nodule is discovered in the left lower lobe in this chest CT scan you can see an interlobar lymph node the pulmonary nodule and the staple line from previous surgery two weeks later a PET scan is performed and the lymph node and the pulmonary nodule are confirmed with a standardized uptake value more than We can also observe some progression in dimensions of the nodule and lymph node.
The surgeon performs a left lower lobectomy, encountering pleural adhesions at the site of the previous mini-thoracotomy. These adhesions are dissected using a blunt instrument or a hook.

The lung is visible after adhesiolysis, with the surgeon preparing to dissect the pulmonary ligament. The station IX lymph node is harvested, and the inferior border of the inferior pulmonary vein is identified.

The surgeon sections the anterior and posterior mediastinal pleura, identifying the superior border of the inferior pulmonary vein. The dissection of the vein is prepared, with plans to staple the vein after completing the arterial dissection to avoid vascular tension.
The surgeon releases the fissure using a hook and scissors, revealing the pulmonary artery within the fissure.
The interlobar lymph node is visible between the lower lobe bronchus and branches of the pulmonary artery, including A-V, A-VI, and the basal truncus.

The surgeon performs a challenging dissection of lymphadenopathy tightly adhered to bronchial and vascular structures, using both blunt dissection and hook electrocautery. Once the arterial branches are cleared of lymph nodes, a 35mm wide end stapler cartridge is applied to complete the vascular dissection for each vessel.

Following arterial stapling, the surgeon exposes the lymph node and surrounding structures, including the left lower bronchus and pulmonary artery. The inferior pulmonary vein is prepared for stapling using a curved tip stapler reload, allowing the stapler to pass beneath the vessel.

The surgeon observes the stump of the inferior pulmonary vein and the left lower lobe bronchus. The next step involves releasing the bronchus by completing the interlobar lymphadenectomy and finalizing the bronchus dissection using a 45mm green endostapler reload.

The surgeon checks the inflation of the upper lobe, indicated by a blue arrow, before firing the stapler. The mediastinal and hilar lymphadenectomy is completed, and a 24 Fr Sharier chest tube is inserted into the pleural cavity through the working incision for drainage.
The postoperative chest x-ray is nearly normal. The patient was discharged three days after surgery.
